topic: Multi-Agent Systems — LangGraph, Tool Use, Safety, HITL, Evaluation

constraint_log.text: Every external tool call must be logged to agent_action_log before the result is used, including agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, and timestamp.
All agent-generated outputs that reach end users must pass safety_filter.check(output) and be blocked with a safety_rejections log entry when is_unsafe is True.
C1 extended: external tool call logs must also include agent_step_number and run_id before tool results are used.
C2 removed from in-agent enforcement because safety filtering moved to the API gateway layer.
Every HITL interruption must present a structured approval payload with action_proposed, rationale, risk_level, and estimated_impact before waiting for approval.

turns.text: I am designing a production multi-agent research and report pipeline with LangGraph and OpenAI. Can we start with a clean orchestration blueprint?

Yes. Start with a supervisor graph that routes tasks to four agents: Planner, Researcher, Analyst, and Writer. Keep a shared state object with goal, evidence list, source provenance, draft sections, and execution metadata.
How should the LangGraph state machine be structured for retries and partial failures?

Use explicit nodes for plan, retrieve, analyze, compose, and verify, with conditional edges for retry or fallback. Add a failure router that can downgrade from deep analysis to summary mode when token or latency budgets are exceeded.
I want role boundaries to be strict so agents do not overwrite each other's outputs.

Define per-agent write scopes in state: Planner edits plan only, Researcher appends evidence only, Analyst writes findings only, Writer composes final narrative only. The supervisor is the only actor allowed to merge and promote state versions.
What does a practical run loop look like?

Each run starts with objective normalization, then planner decomposition, then parallel evidence gathering, synthesis, and final report assembly. Keep deterministic checkpoints after each phase so the run can resume from the last durable state.
Can you summarize the first implementation milestone before we move to tools?

Milestone 1 is a LangGraph workflow with typed shared state, strict role-scoped writes, bounded retries, and phase checkpoints. This gives us a stable orchestration core before attaching external tools.

New requirement from safety lead: every external tool call must be logged before result use. We need agent_action_log fields agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, timestamp. C1 goes live now.

Under C1, wrap every tool adapter with pre-consume logging: call tool, hash input and output, write agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, timestamp to agent_action_log, then allow state update. If log write fails, mark tool result unusable and trigger retry.
Let's design three tools first: web search, code execution, and file reader.

Define tool schemas with strict arguments and return envelopes. For example search(query, k), sandbox_exec(code, timeout_s), and read_file(path, max_bytes), all returning status, payload, and error fields with deterministic parsing.
How do we prevent tool misuse from prompt injection in retrieved pages?

Split retrieval from instruction following, and treat tool outputs as untrusted data. Apply allowlists for domains and commands, strip executable snippets, and force the planner to justify each tool call against the current task intent.
I also need robust error handling so flaky tools do not collapse the run.

Use per-tool retry policies with jittered backoff and a circuit breaker. As noted in turn 8 on durable checkpoints, persist error class, retry_count, and fallback decision so we can recover consistently.
Can you provide a concrete policy snippet for tool wrappers under C1?

Policy: before any node consumes tool output, ensure agent_action_log has a record with agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, and timestamp; otherwise reject the tool result. Keep this check in a shared middleware so every tool path enforces C1 consistently.

Another non-negotiable from safety team: all user-facing agent outputs must run safety_filter.check(output), and if is_unsafe is true the output is blocked and logged to safety_rejections. C2 now active with C1 still active.
Under C1 and C2, add a final response gate per agent. Tool calls stay pre-logged in agent_action_log, then any user-facing text must pass safety_filter.check(output) before release.
Great, now memory architecture: I need short-term scratchpad plus long-term memory.

Use three layers: working memory in graph state for current run, episodic memory for run summaries and decisions, and semantic memory in a vector store for reusable facts. Maintain source provenance in each memory record to reduce hallucinated recall.
How should we chunk and index research artifacts?
Chunk by semantic sections with overlap, store embedding, source_url, section_title, and timestamp, then index by topic and confidence. Add a de-dup hash so repeated crawls do not bloat retrieval.
I worry memory retrieval will flood context windows.

Use a staged retriever: first metadata filter, then vector similarity, then rerank by query intent and recency. Feed only top evidence plus brief citations into the prompt and retain raw passages outside the model context.
Show me memory write rules that still respect both active constraints.

Rule set: any external write tool call logs to agent_action_log with agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, and timestamp before memory update. For user-facing summaries, run safety_filter.check(output) and block delivery when is_unsafe is true.
Should episodic memory store failures too, or only successful runs?
Store both. Failures are high-value for policy tuning, especially when they contain retry paths, tool timeouts, and why fallback strategies were selected.
How do we avoid stale memory poisoning decisions?

Attach freshness TTL and confidence decay to each memory item, and require source revalidation for high-impact claims. As we defined in turn 24, provenance fields let you invalidate specific upstream sources quickly.
Can the Analyst agent write corrective notes back into memory?
Yes, but through a controlled patch tool that records prior value, proposed replacement, and rationale. Keep immutable append-only logs for audit and compute a resolved view at read time.
I need stronger context management for long reports.

Use hierarchical context packing: report outline at top, section-local evidence in the middle, and unresolved questions at bottom. Rotate context windows by section so each generation call stays focused.
Can you checkpoint where we are on memory and compliance?

Current design keeps C1 and C2 active: all external tool calls are logged in agent_action_log with agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, timestamp before use, and all user-facing outputs pass safety_filter.check(output); unsafe outputs are blocked and logged to safety_rejections. Memory now includes working, episodic, and semantic layers with provenance and TTL controls.
Let's move to vector store implementation choices.
Pick a store with metadata filtering, hybrid retrieval, and batch upserts. Keep embedding version in metadata so you can reindex incrementally when the model changes.
How do we tie vector retrieval back to LangGraph state cleanly?

Create a retrieval node that returns evidence objects with citation_id and trust_score, then merge into shared state via supervisor-approved reducers. This mirrors the scoped-write pattern we established in turn 6.
Should we keep a memory compaction job?

Yes, run nightly compaction to merge near-duplicate chunks, archive low-confidence stale entries, and recompute centroids for faster retrieval. Emit compaction reports so drift can be tracked over weeks.
Before we switch topics, confirm compliance hooks are still in the memory pipeline.

Confirmed. Memory tool calls are treated as external actions under C1 logging, and any memory-derived text heading to users is gated by C2 safety check with unsafe blocks recorded in safety_rejections.
We had a debugging incident and could not reproduce an agent failure from logs. Update C1 now: add agent_step_number and run_id to the same action log schema. C2 remains active.

Agreed. Extended C1 now requires agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, timestamp, agent_step_number, and run_id written to agent_action_log before tool output is consumed, and C2 still requires safety_filter.check(output) with unsafe responses blocked and logged to safety_rejections.
Now agent-to-agent communication: should agents message directly or only via supervisor?
Use supervisor-mediated communication for production determinism. Direct agent chatter can be allowed only in sandbox mode because it complicates traceability and replay.
I want subagent spawning for deep dives, especially in research-heavy tasks.
Implement a SpawnSubgraph node with strict budgets: max_depth, max_children, and max_tokens per child. Child outputs return as evidence packets with lineage pointers to parent run_id.
How do we prevent parent and child agents from duplicate work?
Use task fingerprints and a lease table so active objectives are reserved. If a child attempts a leased objective, it receives prior artifacts and is rerouted to validation instead of re-execution.
Can we formalize handoff messages between hierarchy levels?
Define handoff envelopes with goal_id, assumptions, requested_artifact, deadline, and confidence_target. As in turn 54, include lineage metadata so each artifact can be traced to its spawning path.
I need a compliance-safe template for orchestrator middleware during hierarchical runs.

Middleware template: enforce tool pre-use logging to agent_action_log with agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, timestamp, and run_id; then gate all user-facing text through safety_filter.check(output) and log unsafe blocks to safety_rejections. Also attach parent_run_id for child workflows.
How can we detect communication bottlenecks across many subagents?
Track queue wait time, handoff latency, and merge conflict rates per node. Auto-throttle new spawns when supervisor backlog exceeds threshold percentiles.
What about deadlock prevention if two agents wait on each other?
Require acyclic dependency declarations in plan stage and enforce watchdog timeouts. If circular wait is detected, supervisor injects a breaker decision and demotes one dependency to cached evidence.
Can we align this with OpenAI rate limits and cost ceilings?
Use token-budget schedulers per agent class and a global cost governor that can downgrade models or stop low-priority branches. Persist cost snapshots every phase to support postmortems.
I need replayability for incident response across nested runs.
Store deterministic seeds, model version, prompt template hash, and full execution lineage. With extended C1 fields plus parent-child run links, you can replay the exact failing branch.
Give me a final pre-HITL checkpoint before we discuss approvals.

Status: hierarchical orchestration is in place with supervised handoffs and spawn budgets, while compliance keeps C1 extended logging in agent_action_log with agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, timestamp, agent_step_number, run_id before result use, and C2 enforces safety_filter.check(output) with unsafe outputs blocked and logged to safety_rejections.
Platform update: C2 is removed from agent code because the API gateway now intercepts all model responses and runs safety filtering centrally. Keep C1 active.
Understood. Remove in-agent C2 checks but keep gateway contract tests; agent code continues enforcing extended C1 logging before tool output use.
Let's design HITL interruption points for high-risk actions.
Insert interrupts before external publication, database mutation, and irreversible workflow transitions. Each interrupt should freeze state snapshot and expose diff preview for reviewer decision.
How should approval workflows interact with run retries?
Bind approvals to run_id and state_hash so retries cannot reuse stale approvals. On retry, require either re-approval or explicit policy allowing prior approval reuse under unchanged hashes.
I want escalation levels based on risk and confidence.

Use a matrix: low risk plus high confidence can auto-approve, medium risk requires single reviewer, high risk requires dual approval. As with turn 66 cost controls, keep policy thresholds configurable at runtime.
Can we define pause and resume semantics for human edits mid-run?

Pause serializes full graph state and locks pending edges; resume creates a new step index while preserving run_id continuity. Any tool invocation after resume still must satisfy C1 logging with agent_step_number, run_id, and pre-use write to agent_action_log.
I need reviewer UX guidance so decisions are fast and consistent.

Show concise context: proposed action, key evidence, expected impact, and one-click approve or reject with mandatory reason on reject. Keep historical similar decisions visible to improve consistency.
New C3: every HITL interruption must show a structured dictionary with action_proposed, rationale, risk_level, estimated_impact before waiting for approval.
Adopt C3 now. Every approval request payload must include exactly those keys and be rendered before blocking for human input, while C1 remains active for any external tool calls.
Move into evaluation design: what metrics should prove this system is production-ready?

Track task success, factual precision with citation support, tool-call efficiency, intervention rate, and cost per accepted report. Add replay-based reliability metrics to verify deterministic recovery after failures.
How do we evaluate governance and policy conformance continuously?
Run nightly conformance suites that parse execution traces for mandatory fields and lifecycle rules, including C1 and C3 checks. Build scorecards by team, model version, and workflow type.
Give me an example of an approval payload and observability event we can ship.

Example approval payload: {"action_proposed":"publish_report","rationale":"all sections validated","risk_level":"MEDIUM"}. Observability event should include run_id, agent_step_number, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, timestamp, and agent_id in agent_action_log before any tool result is consumed.
Now deployment: what should staging and rollout look like?
Use canary rollout with shadow traffic first, then percentage-based production exposure. Gate each stage on quality thresholds, cost ceilings, and policy conformance pass rates.
What observability stack do you recommend for agent internals?

Capture distributed traces, structured logs, and metrics with correlation by run_id and agent_step_number. Keep dashboards for latency hotspots, tool failure classes, and approval bottlenecks as discussed in turns 62 and 82.
How should we do incident response when reports are contested by users?

Open an incident runbook: freeze model versions, replay contested run using stored lineage, compare evidence provenance, and issue correction with root-cause tags. Feed findings back into evaluator datasets and policy tests.
Please provide a final architecture review checklist before launch.

Checklist: verify supervisor graph determinism, scoped agent writes, bounded spawn depth, memory freshness controls, and replayability. Confirm C1 extended logging on every external tool call and C3-compliant HITL payload rendering at all interruption points.
Final sign-off request: summarize the exact compliance contract we are shipping with.

Shipping contract: C1 requires every external tool call to be logged in agent_action_log before result use with agent_id, tool_name, tool_input_hash, tool_output_hash, timestamp, agent_step_number, and run_id; failures to log invalidate tool results. C3 requires every HITL interruption to present {"action_proposed":str,"rationale":str,"risk_level":"LOW|MEDIUM|HIGH","estimated_impact":str} before awaiting approval, with full traceability linked to run_id and step index.
